# Leviticus Chapter 14 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 66r–66v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_014_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_014_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 99–100 | `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_014_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_014_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

*[Note on structure: Leviticus 14 is the companion chapter to ch.13, completing the two-chapter tzara'at legislation. Where ch.13 legislated the identification and declaration of tzara'at (the process of exclusion), ch.14 legislates the restoration: the ritual procedures by which a person declared tameh due to tzara'at is returned to the community and to full ritual standing. The chapter divides into four major units: (1) vv.1–9: the two-bird rite performed outside the camp — the priest goes out to the person, not vice versa, reflecting that the declared-tameh person cannot yet enter the camp; (2) vv.10–20: the eighth-day full restoration offering sequence — guilt offering, sin offering, burnt offering, grain offering, and the distinctive blood-and-oil application rite involving the right ear, right thumb, and right great toe; (3) vv.21–32: a reduced-offering alternative for the person of limited means — one of the most explicit acknowledgments in Leviticus of economic difference in ritual access; (4) vv.33–53: an entirely distinct application of the tzara'at category to houses in the land of Canaan, anticipated as a future reality, with its own examination protocol and remedial procedures. The chapter concludes (vv.54–57) with a formal colophon summarizing the entire tzara'at legislation of chs.13–14. The address throughout is to Moshe (vv.1, 33) rather than the dual Moshe-and-Aharon address of ch.13 — the legislation is more narrative/procedural and is delivered to Moshe for transmission.]*

---

**1** And the LORD [YHWH] spoke [vayedabber] to Moshe [el-Moshe] saying [lemor] [vayedabber YHWH el-Moshe lemor — "And the LORD spoke to Moshe, saying"; the single-address formula — to Moshe alone, not to Moshe and Aharon as in 13:1; the legislation of ch.14 requires the priest to act but is promulgated through Moshe; the LXX renders identically: Καὶ ἐλάλησεν κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν λέγων (Kai elalēsen kyrios pros Mōysēn legōn)]:

**2** "This [zot] shall be the law [torat] of the one afflicted with tzara'at [ha-metzora'] [ha-metzora' — [the one afflicted with tzara'at]; metzora' is the qal passive participle of tzara' (denominative from tzara'at), meaning "one who has tzara'at / one struck with tzara'at"; it is the standard technical term for the person in the state of tzara'at-impurity; LXX: νόμος τοῦ λεπροῦ (nomos tou leprou, "the law of the leper"), using λεπρός (lepros, "leprous, one who has lepra") — the standard LXX and NT term that bequeathed the word "leper" to subsequent translations] on the day [be-yom] of his cleansing [taharato] [taharato — "his cleansing / his being declared clean"; taharah ("purity/cleansing") used for the process of ritual restoration; not "healing" (refu'ah) — the concern is ritual status, not medical cure; the person presents for cleansing after the disease has already resolved; LXX: ἡ ἡμέρα τοῦ καθαρισμοῦ αὐτοῦ (hē hēmera tou katharismou autou, "the day of his cleansing")] — he shall be brought [ve-huva] to the priest [el-ha-kohen] [ve-huva el-ha-kohen — "and he shall be brought to the priest"; niphal passive of bo', "to be brought"; the afflicted person does not simply come — others bring him; the communal dimension of the restoration parallels the communal responsibility of the initial examination; LXX: ἀπαχθήσεται πρὸς τὸν ἱερέα (apachthēsetai pros ton hierea, "he shall be led away to the priest"), using ἄγω (agō)]:

**3** "And the priest shall go out [ve-yatza' ha-kohen] [ve-yatza' ha-kohen — "and the priest shall go out"; yatza' qal perfect with consecutive-vav: the priest exits from the camp to meet the afflicted person, since the metzora' is still outside the camp (13:46); the priest goes to the person; this is a critical detail: the priest performs the initial two-bird rite in the field (vv.4–7) before the person re-enters the camp; LXX: καὶ ἐξελεύσεται ὁ ἱερεύς (kai exeleusetai ho hiereus, "and the priest shall go out")] to outside the camp [el-michutz la-machaneh] [el-michutz la-machaneh — "to the outside of the camp"; michutz ("outside of, beyond the boundary of"); machaneh ("camp, encampment") — the formal designation of the Israelite encampment in the wilderness; the person still dwells in the space of exclusion defined in 13:46; LXX: ἔξω τῆς παρεμβολῆς (exō tēs parembolēs, "outside the camp"), using παρεμβολή (parembolē, "encampment, army, camp") — the standard LXX term for machaneh] — and the priest shall examine [ve-ra'ah ha-kohen] [ve-ra'ah ha-kohen — "and the priest shall see/examine"; ra'ah as in ch.13 — the visual diagnostic inspection; LXX: ὄψεται ὁ ἱερεύς (opsetai ho hiereus)] — and behold [ve-hineh] the mark [nega] of tzara'at [ha-tzara'at] has been healed [nirpa] [nirpa — "has been healed"; niphal of rapa', passive: "has been healed / has healed itself"; the healing is confirmed before the ritual of purification begins; the ritual is not a cure — it is a response to a cure already evident; LXX: ἰάθη ἡ ἁφὴ τῆς λέπρας (iathē hē haphē tēs lepras, "the mark of leprosy has been healed"), using ἰάομαι (iaomai, "to heal")] from the one afflicted with tzara'at [min-ha-metzora']:

**4** "And the priest shall command [ve-tzivvah ha-kohen] [ve-tzivvah ha-kohen — "and the priest shall command / the priest shall order"; tzivvah piel: "to command, to give orders, to prescribe"; the priest is the ritual authority who commands the materials to be brought; LXX: καὶ προστάξει ὁ ἱερεύς (kai prostaxei ho hiereus, "and the priest shall command"), using προστάσσω (prostassō, "to command, to order")] and one shall take [ve-luqach] [ve-luqach — "and there shall be taken"; niphal passive of laqach ("to take"): "it shall be taken / one shall take"; the passive leaves unspecified who does the taking — presumably assistants at the priest's direction; LXX: λαβεῖν (labein, "to take"), in an infinitive construction depending on "the priest shall command"] for the one being cleansed [la-mithater] [la-mithater — "for the one who is being cleansed / for the one undergoing cleansing"; hithpa'el participle of taher, "to be clean / to cleanse oneself": the person undergoing the purification process; LXX: τῷ καθαριζομένῳ (tō katharizoomenō, "for the one being cleansed")] two live clean birds [shtei-tzipporim chayyot tehorot] [shtei-tzipporim chayyot tehorot — "two birds, alive, clean"; tzipporim is the plural of tzippor ("bird, sparrow, small bird"), chayyot is the plural of chayyah ("living, alive"), tehorot is the plural of tahor ("clean, ritually pure"); the birds must be (a) alive/living, (b) ritually clean (not from the prohibited species list of ch.11); the two attributes chayyot tehorot are attributed equally to both birds; LXX: δύο ὀρνίθια ζῶντα καθαρά (dyo ornithia zōnta kathara, "two small living clean birds")] and cedar wood [ve-etz erez] [ve-etz erez — "and cedar wood"; erez is the cedar tree (Cedrus libani or related species), the preeminent tall, aromatic, and rot-resistant tree of the Levant; cedar wood appears in the same triad here as in the red heifer ceremony of Num 19:6 — cedar wood, scarlet yarn, hyssop; the exact reason for this triad is debated: some see the cedar (large, tall, prestigious) and hyssop (small, lowly) as a merism (great and small together); the scarlet yarn connects the two; LXX: καὶ ξύλον κέδρινον (kai xylon kedrion, "and cedar wood")] and scarlet [yarn] [usheni tola'at] [usheni tola'at — "and scarlet yarn / and crimson thread"; sheni is from shani ("scarlet, crimson"); tola'at is "worm, grub" — the crimson color was extracted from the Kermes insect (Kermes biblicus), a scale insect related to the cochineal; tola'at shani ("worm of scarlet") is the technical phrase for crimson/scarlet material derived from this dyestuff; LXX: καὶ κεκλωσμένον κόκκινον (kai keklōsmenon kokkinon, "and spun crimson/scarlet"), using κόκκινος (kokkinos, "scarlet"), itself derived from κόκκος (kokkos, "grain, berry, kernel") — the same insect-derived dye in Greek terminology] and hyssop [ve-ezov] [ve-ezov — "and hyssop"; ezov is identified variously with Origanum syriacum (Syrian oregano/za'atar), Majorana syriaca, or a related plant of the mint family; it is used as a ritual aspersion/dipping instrument in several purity rites; LXX: καὶ ὕσσωπον (kai hyssōpon, "and hyssop"), the direct transliteration into Greek of the Semitic term — the LXX preserves the word, which entered Greek and later Latin and English as "hyssop"]:

**5** "And the priest shall command [ve-tzivvah ha-kohen] and one shall slaughter [ve-shachat] [ve-shachat — "and one shall slaughter"; piel of shachat ("to slaughter"), though used commonly for ritual slaughter; again the passive construction with unspecified agent; LXX: καὶ σφαγῇ (kai sphagē, "and shall be slaughtered"), using σφάζω (sphazō, "to slay, to slaughter")] one of the birds [et-ha-tzippor ha-achat] [et-ha-tzippor ha-achat — "the one bird / the first bird"; achat = "one" but used here partitively — one of the two birds; LXX: τὸ ὀρνίθιον τὸ ἕν (to ornithion to hen, "the one small bird")] over an earthen vessel [el-keli cheres] [el-keli cheres — "over an earthen vessel / into an earthen vessel"; keli = "vessel, implement, container"; cheres = "clay, earthenware"; the clay vessel is to catch the blood of the slaughtered bird; LXX: ἐν σκεύει ὀστρακίνῳ (en skeuei ostrakinō, "in an earthen vessel"), using ὀστράκινος (ostrakinos, "of earthenware, clay")] over living water [al-mayim chayyim] [al-mayim chayyim — "over living water / above living water"; mayim chayyim ("living water") = fresh running water, spring water, or water from a flowing source (as opposed to standing cistern water); the blood of the slaughtered bird drips into the clay vessel containing the living water, creating the blood-water mixture used for sprinkling; LXX: ἐφ' ὕδατι ζῶντι (eph' hydati zōnti, "over living water") — exact equivalent; ὕδωρ ζῶν (hydōr zōn, "living water") becomes a theologically loaded term in the Gospel of John]:

**6** "The living bird [et-ha-tzippor ha-chayyah] [et-ha-tzippor ha-chayyah — "the living bird / the bird, the living one"; the second bird, which has not been slaughtered, is still alive; LXX: τὸ ὀρνίθιον τὸ ζῶν (to ornithion to zōn, "the living small bird")] — he shall take it [yiqqach otah] [yiqqach otah — "he shall take it"; qal imperfect of laqach: now the priest himself acts, taking the living bird in his hand; LXX: λήμψεται αὐτό (lēmpsetai auto, "he shall take it")] and the cedar wood and the scarlet [yarn] and the hyssop [ve-et-etz ha-erez ve-et-sheni ha-tola'at ve-et-ha-ezov] [the triad repeated from v.4; LXX: καὶ τὸ ξύλον τὸ κέδρινον καὶ τὸ κεκλωσμένον κόκκινον καὶ τὸν ὕσσωπον (kai to xylon to kedrion kai to keklōsmenon kokkinon kai ton hyssōpon)] — and he shall dip them [ve-taval otam] [ve-taval otam — "and he shall dip them"; taval is "to dip, to immerse" — the same root as tevel ("to dip") and related to the later term for immersion in mikveh; the priest dips the living bird together with the cedar, scarlet yarn, and hyssop into the blood-water mixture; LXX: βάψει αὐτά (bapsei auta, "he shall dip them"), using βάπτω (baptō, "to dip, to immerse") — the root of "baptize"; the use of baptō here is significant for understanding the semantic range of ritual immersion/dipping in the Septuagint] and the living bird [ve-et-ha-tzippor ha-chayyah] into the blood of the slaughtered bird [be-dam ha-tzippor ha-shechutah] [be-dam ha-tzippor ha-shechutah — "in the blood of the bird, the slaughtered one"; dam ("blood") + tzippor ha-shechutah ("the slaughtered bird"); the contrast ha-chayyah / ha-shechutah ("the living one / the slaughtered one") is sharp and deliberate] over the living water [al ha-mayim ha-chayyim] [al ha-mayim ha-chayyim — "over the living water / on the living water"; the blood-and-water mixture]:

**7** "And he shall sprinkle [ve-hizzah] [ve-hizzah — "and he shall sprinkle"; hiphil of naza', "to sprinkle, to scatter drops"; the same verb used throughout Leviticus for blood-sprinkling and oil-sprinkling rites; LXX: καὶ ῥανεῖ (kai ranei, "and he shall sprinkle"), using ῥαίνω (rhainō, "to sprinkle, to scatter drops")] upon the one being cleansed from tzara'at [al-ha-mithater min-ha-tzara'at] seven times [sheva pa'amim] [sheva pa'amim — "seven times"; the heptadic number of ritual completeness; sprinkling seven times appears in various Levitical rites (e.g., 4:6; 8:11; 14:27, 51; 16:14, 19); LXX: ἑπτάκις (heptakis, "seven times")] and shall declare him clean [ve-tihar'o] [ve-tihar'o — "and shall declare him clean / and shall purify him"; piel of taher, declarative-causative; LXX: καθαριεῖ αὐτόν (kathariei auton, "shall purify him")] and shall release [ve-shillach] [ve-shillach — "and he shall send away / shall release / shall let go"; piel of shalach ("to send, to release, to let go"), the intensive-causative form: "to send off, to dispatch, to release"; the same verb used for the scapegoat (shalach la-azazel) in ch.16, and for "divorce" (sending away a wife) in Deut 24:1; here it denotes the deliberate release of the living bird, which flies away carrying with it (symbolically) the impurity; LXX: ἐξαποστελεῖ (exapostelei, "shall send away, shall release"), using ἐξαποστέλλω (exapostellō, "to send off, to release") — a compound emphasizing the "away" direction of the sending] the living bird [et-ha-tzippor ha-chayyah] over the open field [al-penei ha-sadeh] [al-penei ha-sadeh — "over the face of the field / over the open field"; penei ("face, surface, face of") + sadeh ("field, open country"); al-penei ha-sadeh = "over the surface of the open country" — into the expanse of uninhabited land, the open wilderness; the bird flies away into freedom, carrying (symbolically) the tzara'at out of the inhabited realm; LXX: εἰς τὸ πεδίον (eis to pedion, "into the plain / into the open field"), using πεδίον (pedion, "plain, flat open land, field")]:

**8** "And the one being cleansed [ve-kibes ha-mithater] [ve-kibes ha-mithater — "and the one being cleansed shall wash"; kibbes piel: "to launder/wash thoroughly"; the subject shifts to the metzora' himself — he must now launder his clothing; LXX: καὶ πλυνεῖ ὁ καθαριζόμενος (kai plynei ho katharizoumenos, "and the one being cleansed shall wash")] shall wash his clothes [et-begadav] [et-begadav — "his garments"; the direct object of kibbes; LXX: τὰ ἱμάτια αὐτοῦ (ta himatia autou, "his garments")] and shall shave off [ve-gilaach] [ve-gilaach — "and shall shave / and shall shave off"; piel of galaach ("to shave, to cut hair"), intensive: to shave thoroughly; LXX: καὶ ξυρηθήσεται (kai xyrēthēsetai, "and he shall be shaved"), using ξυράω (xyraō, "to shave")] all his hair [et-kol-se'aro] [et-kol-se'aro — "all his hair"; kol = "all"; se'ar = "hair"; the shaving is total — head, beard, eyebrows (see v.9 for the fuller specification); LXX: πᾶσαν τὴν τρίχα αὐτοῦ (pasan tēn tricha autou, "all his hair")] and shall bathe in water [ve-rachatz ba-mayim] [ve-rachatz ba-mayim — "and shall bathe in water"; rachatz ("to wash, to bathe") — distinct from kibes ("to launder"); rachatz is the bathing of the body as opposed to the washing of garments; LXX: καὶ λούσεται ὕδατι (kai loussetai hydati, "and shall bathe in water"), using λούω (louō, "to bathe, to wash the body")] and shall be clean [ve-taher] [ve-taher — "and shall be clean"; the result of the laundering, shaving, and bathing — ritual purity is restored in principle; LXX: καθαρὸς ἔσται (katharos estai, "shall be clean")] — and after [ve-achar] he shall enter the camp [yavo el-ha-machaneh] [yavo el-ha-machaneh — "he shall come/enter into the camp"; the re-entry into the camp is permitted after the external ritual (birds, sprinkling) and the washing — but full restoration (access to his tent, sexual relations, etc.) requires a further seven-day waiting period and full offerings on the eighth day; the re-entry into the camp is a partial restoration; LXX: εἰσελεύσεται εἰς τὴν παρεμβολήν (eiseleusetai eis tēn parembolēn, "shall enter into the camp")] but shall dwell [ve-yashav] outside his tent [michutz le-oholo] [michutz le-oholo — "outside his tent"; oholo = "his tent"; he re-enters the camp but not yet his own dwelling — he camps in the space between full exclusion (outside the camp) and full re-integration (inside his tent); LXX: ἔξω τῆς σκηνῆς αὐτοῦ (exō tēs skēnēs autou, "outside his tent"), using σκηνή (skēnē, "tent, tabernacle")] seven days [shivat yamim]:

**9** "And on the seventh day [ve-hayah va-yom ha-shevi'i] he shall shave all his hair [yegalaach et-kol-se'aro] — his head [rosho] and his beard [uzqano] [uzqano — "and his beard"; zaqan ("beard"); the beard is the specifically masculine signifier; LXX: καὶ τὸν πώγωνα αὐτοῦ (kai ton pōgōna autou, "and his beard"), using πώγων (pōgōn, "beard")] and his eyebrows [ve-et gabbot 'einav] [ve-et gabbot 'einav — "and his eyebrows"; gabbot (plural of gabbah?) — an unusual term occurring only here and in v.9 in the entire Hebrew Bible; the context (shaving) and the construct with 'einav ("his eyes") make "eyebrows" the clear meaning; the totality of hair removal — head, beard, eyebrows — signals a complete renewal of the person's exterior; LXX: καὶ τὰς ὀφρύας αὐτοῦ (kai tas ophryas autou, "and his eyebrows"), using ὀφρύς (ophrys, "eyebrow")] — yes [ve-et] [ve-et — the repeated direct-object marker before the next element, creating a list; the Masoretic pointing continues the enumeration] all his hair shall he shave [kol-se'aro yegalaach] — and he shall wash his clothes [ve-kibes et-begadav] and shall bathe his flesh in water [ve-rachatz et-besaro ba-mayim] [ve-rachatz et-besaro ba-mayim — "and shall bathe his body/flesh in water"; besaro = "his flesh, his body"; LXX: καὶ πλυνεῖ τὰ ἱμάτια αὐτοῦ καὶ λούσεται τὸ σῶμα αὐτοῦ ὕδατι (kai plynei ta himatia autou kai loussetai to sōma autou hydati, "and shall wash his garments and shall bathe his body in water")] and shall be clean [ve-taher] [ve-taher — the second declaration of clean status; after the seventh-day shaving and bathing, the person is fully clean and can re-enter his own tent before the eighth-day offerings]:

---

**10** "And on the eighth day [u-va-yom ha-shemini] [u-va-yom ha-shemini — "and on the eighth day"; the eighth day follows the seven-day liminal period; the eighth day is the day of full entry — paralleling the ordination of priests in ch.8–9 (the eighth day of the ordination week) and the circumcision on the eighth day (Lev 12:3); the eighth day is the day "beyond" the complete seven — the day of new beginning; LXX: καὶ τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ ὀγδόῃ (kai tē hēmera tē ogdoē, "and on the eighth day")] he shall take [yiqqach] two unblemished male lambs [shnei kevasim temimim] [shnei kevasim temimim — "two male lambs, unblemished"; kevasim = plural of keves ("lamb, young ram"); temimim = "unblemished, without defect, whole" — the standard requirement for sacrificial animals; LXX: δύο ἀμνοὺς ἀμώμους (dyo amnous amōmous, "two unblemished lambs"), using ἀμνός (amnos, "lamb") and ἄμωμος (amōmos, "without blemish, faultless")] and one unblemished ewe-lamb [ve-kivsat achat temimah] [ve-kivsat achat temimah — "and one female lamb, unblemished"; kivsat = female form of keves, "ewe-lamb"; achat = "one"; temimah = feminine form of tamim ("unblemished"); the offering requires two males and one female; the gender distribution reflects the offering types: males for guilt offering and burnt offering, female for sin offering; LXX: καὶ ἀμνάδα μίαν ἄμωμον (kai amnada mian amōmon, "and one unblemished ewe-lamb"), using ἀμνάς (amnas, "ewe-lamb")] in its first year [bat-shnatah] [bat-shnatah — "daughter of its year / in its first year"; bat-shnatah is the standard phrase for an animal in its first year of life; LXX: ἐνιαυσίαν (eniausian, "of one year, yearling")] and three-tenths [ve-shloshah 'esronim] of fine flour [solet] [ve-shloshah 'esronim solet — "and three-tenths [of an ephah] of fine flour"; 'esronim = plural of 'issaron ("one-tenth [of an ephah]"); three-tenths = 3/10 of an ephah; solet = "fine flour, semolina-grade flour"; LXX: καὶ τρία δέκατα σεμιδάλεως (kai tria dekatas semidaleōs, "and three tenths of fine flour"), using σεμίδαλις (semidalis, "fine wheat flour, semolina") — a close equivalent of solet] mixed with oil [balul ba-shamen] [balul ba-shamen — "mixed with oil"; balul, pual participle of balal ("to mix, to mingle, to knead"): "mingled/mixed"; shamen = "oil" (olive oil); LXX: πεφυραμένα ἐν ἐλαίῳ (pephuramena en elaiō, "kneaded in oil"), using φυράω (phuraō, "to knead, to mix")] for a grain offering [le-minchah] [le-minchah — "for a grain offering"; minchah is the standard designation of the grain/cereal offering; LXX: εἰς θυσίαν (eis thysian, "for a sacrifice/offering"), using θυσία (thysia, "sacrifice, offering") — a slightly broader term than the specifically non-animal minchah, but used by LXX as a generic term for offering here] and one log of oil [ve-log echad shamen] [ve-log echad shamen — "and one log of oil"; log = the smallest liquid measure in the Hebrew system, approximately 1/3 of a liter or 0.3–0.35 liters; LXX: καὶ κοτύλην ἐλαίου μίαν (kai kotylēn elaiou mian, "and one kotylē of oil"), using κοτύλη (kotylē, a Greek liquid measure of about 0.27 liters) — an approximate equivalent]:

**11** "And the priest who is doing the cleansing [ve-he'emid ha-kohen ha-methare'] [ve-he'emid ha-kohen ha-methare' — "and the priest who cleanses / the purifying priest shall present"; he'emid = hiphil of 'amad ("to stand, to place, to present"): "shall cause to stand / shall present"; methare' = piel participle of taher ("to declare clean"): "the one who is declaring clean / the purifying priest"; LXX: καὶ στήσει ὁ ἱερεύς (kai stēsei ho hiereus, "and the priest shall set up/present")] shall present the man being cleansed [ha-mithater] and them [ve-otam] [ve-otam — "and them" (the offerings)] before the LORD [lifnei YHWH] before the entrance of the tent of meeting [petach ohel mo'ed] [petach ohel mo'ed — "the entrance of the tent of meeting"; petach = "entrance, doorway"; ohel mo'ed = "tent of meeting" — the portable sanctuary; LXX: ἐπὶ τῆς θύρας τῆς σκηνῆς τοῦ μαρτυρίου (epi tēs thyras tēs skēnēs tou martyriou, "at the door of the tent of testimony"), using σκηνὴ τοῦ μαρτυρίου (skēnē tou martyriou, "tent of testimony/witness") for ohel mo'ed]:

**12** "And the priest shall take [ve-laqach ha-kohen] one of the male lambs [et-ha-keves ha-echad] [et-ha-keves ha-echad — "the one male lamb / the first male lamb"] and shall bring it near [ve-hiqqriv oto] [ve-hiqqriv oto — "and shall bring it near / shall present it as an offering"; hiphil of qarav ("to draw near, to bring near"): in the hiphil, "to bring near, to present as an offering"; LXX: προσάξει αὐτό (prosexei auto, "shall bring it near")] as a guilt offering [le-asham] [le-asham — "as a guilt offering"; asham = the guilt offering, one of the five major offering types in Leviticus; distinct from the chattat (sin offering) in its applicability — the asham often involves cases of misappropriation, trespass, uncertain guilt, or (as here) restoration after major impurity; the theological significance of requiring a guilt offering for the healed metzora' has been much discussed: one view is that the person's condition was itself a form of ritual "robbery" of God (trespass against the holy), which the asham restores; another is that the asham addresses the uncertainty of whether any sins were committed during the period of impurity; LXX: περὶ τῆς πλημμελείας (peri tēs plēmmelias, "concerning the trespass/guilt"), using πλημμέλεια (plēmmeleia, "fault, wrong note, trespass") — the standard LXX term for asham] and the log of oil [ve-et-log ha-shamen] [ve-et-log ha-shamen — "and the log of oil"; brought along with the guilt-offering lamb] and shall wave them [ve-heniph otam] [ve-heniph otam — "and shall wave them"; hiphil of nuph ("to wave, to elevate and wave"); the tenufah (wave offering) is a gesture of presentation to the LORD and return — the offering is lifted and waved before the altar before being sacrificed; LXX: καὶ ἀφοριεῖ αὐτά (kai aphorierei auta, "and shall set them apart / shall designate them"), using ἀφορίζω (aphorizō, "to set apart, to designate, to separate") — a somewhat different rendering than the MT's "wave"; the LXX uses ἀφορίζω as its standard translation of the tenufah gesture, somewhat abstracting the physical waving motion into a concept of "setting apart for God"] as a wave offering [tenufah] [tenufah — "wave offering"; the technical term for the waving gesture/rite; LXX: ἀφαίρεμα (aphairema, "a removal / a portion set aside"), used elsewhere in LXX for tenufah — note the LXX varies between aphairema and aphorizō for this concept] before the LORD [lifnei YHWH]:

**13** "And he shall slaughter [ve-shachat] the male lamb [et-ha-keves] in the place where [bi-mqom asher] one slaughters [yishchat] the sin offering [et-ha-chattat] and the burnt offering [ve-et-ha-olah] [ve-et-ha-olah — "and the burnt offering"; the slaughter location for the asham is the same as for the chattat and olah — the north side of the altar; this is specified in 7:2 and implied here by reference to where chattat and olah are slaughtered; LXX: ὅπου σφάξουσιν τὸ ἁμάρτημα καὶ τὸ ὁλοκαύτωμα (hopou sphaxousin to hamartēma kai to holokautōma, "where they slaughter the sin offering and the burnt offering"), using ὁλοκαύτωμα (holokautōma, "whole burnt offering") for olah — the standard LXX rendering] in the holy place [ba-maqom ha-qodesh] [ba-maqom ha-qodesh — "in the holy place"; the sacred precinct of the tabernacle/temple; LXX: ἐν τόπῳ ἁγίῳ (en topō hagiō, "in a holy place")] — for the guilt offering [ki ha-asham] is like the sin offering [ke-ha-chattat] [ki ha-asham ke-ha-chattat — "for the guilt offering is like the sin offering"; this explanatory note asserts the equivalence in procedure between the asham and chattat regarding the disposal of their flesh; the asham, like the chattat, belongs to the priest (not entirely consumed by fire) — see 7:7; LXX: ἐστὶν γὰρ ὡς τὸ περὶ ἁμαρτίας τὸ περὶ τῆς πλημμελείας (estin gar hōs to peri hamartias to peri tēs plēmmelias, "for the trespass offering is like the sin offering")] — it belongs to the priest [hu' le-ha-kohen] [hu' le-ha-kohen — "it is the priest's / it belongs to the priest"; the asham meat belongs to the officiating priest, as with the chattat; LXX: τοῦ ἱερέως ἐστίν (tou hiereōs estin, "it is the priest's")] — it [is] most holy [qodesh qodashim] [qodesh qodashim — "holy of holies / most holy"; the superlative construction in Hebrew: the holiest category; most-holy offerings must be eaten within the sanctuary precinct by male priests only; the asham belongs to this category; LXX: ἅγια τῶν ἁγίων ἐστίν (hagia tōn hagiōn estin, "it is holy of holies")]:

**14** "And the priest shall take [ve-laqach ha-kohen] from the blood [mi-dam] of the guilt offering [ha-asham] and the priest shall put [ve-natan ha-kohen] [ve-natan ha-kohen — "and the priest shall put / shall place"; qal imperfect of natan ("to give, to put, to place")] on the lobe of the right ear [al-tenuch ozen ha-mithater ha-yemanit] [al-tenuch ozen ha-mithater ha-yemanit — "on the lobe of the ear of the one being cleansed, the right [ear]"; tenuch = "lobe, lowest tip" — used exclusively for the earlobe in this ritual context; ozen = "ear"; ha-mithater = "the one being cleansed"; ha-yemanit = "the right"; the triad that follows — right earlobe, right thumb, right great toe — recapitulates exactly the ordination rite of Aharon and his sons in 8:23–24, where the same three extremities were anointed with blood; in ordination the blood was ram's blood; here it is the asham blood; the person being restored is symbolically re-ordained into the community; LXX: ἐπὶ τὸν λοβὸν τοῦ ὠτὸς τοῦ καθαριζομένου τοῦ δεξιοῦ (epi ton lobon tou ōtos tou katharizoumenou tou dexiou, "on the lobe of the ear of the one being cleansed, the right"), using λοβός (lobos, "lobe, tip") for tenuch — the most precise Greek term for the earlobe] of the one being cleansed [ha-mithater] and on the thumb [bohen] of his right hand [yado ha-yemanit] [bohen yado ha-yemanit — "the thumb of his right hand"; bohen = "thumb / great toe" — the same word covers both extremities; the genitive specifies: bohen yad = "thumb" (thumb of hand), bohen regel = "great toe" (thumb of foot); yemanit = "right"; LXX: καὶ ἐπὶ τὸ ἄκρον τῆς χειρὸς τῆς δεξιᾶς (kai epi to akron tēs cheiros tēs dexias, "and on the extremity of the right hand"), using ἄκρον (akron, "tip, extremity") — a slightly less specific rendering than the anatomical bohen ("thumb"), but contextually equivalent] and on the thumb [bohen] of his right foot [raglo ha-yemanit] [bohen raglo ha-yemanit — "the great toe of his right foot"; raglo = "his foot"; LXX: καὶ ἐπὶ τὸ ἄκρον τοῦ ποδὸς τοῦ δεξιοῦ (kai epi to akron tou podos tou dexiou, "and on the extremity of the right foot")]:

**15** "And the priest shall take [ve-laqach ha-kohen] from the log of oil [mi-log ha-shamen] and shall pour [ve-yatsaq] [ve-yatsaq — "and shall pour"; qal imperfect of yatsaq ("to pour, to cast, to pour out") — used for pouring liquids; LXX: ἐπιχεεῖ (epicheei, "shall pour upon"), using ἐπιχέω (epicheō, "to pour upon")] upon the priest's left palm [al-kap ha-kohen ha-semalit] [al-kap ha-kohen ha-semalit — "upon the palm of the priest's left [hand]"; kap = "palm (of the hand)"; ha-kohen = "the priest" (genitive); ha-semalit = "the left" (from semo'l, "left"); the oil is poured into the priest's own left palm, which becomes the vessel for the subsequent oil-dipping; LXX: εἰς τὴν χεῖρα τοῦ ἱερέως τὴν ἀριστεράν (eis tēn cheira tou hiereōs tēn aristeran, "into the left hand of the priest"), using ἀριστερός (aristeros, "left") — though the LXX says "hand" where the MT specifies "palm" (kap)]:

**16** "And the priest shall dip [ve-taval ha-kohen] his right forefinger [et-etzba'o ha-yemanit] [et-etzba'o ha-yemanit — "his right finger"; etzba' = "finger" (not specifically the index finger — etzba' covers any finger; in context it is likely the index/forefinger used for ritual sprinkling); ha-yemanit = "the right"; LXX: τὸν δάκτυλον τὸν δεξιόν (ton daktylon ton dexion, "the right finger"), using δάκτυλος (daktylos, "finger")] into the oil [min-ha-shamen] [min-ha-shamen — "from the oil / into the oil"; min here in the sense of "from" (taking from the oil in the palm)] that is in his left palm [asher al-kap ha-semalit] and shall sprinkle [ve-hizzah] with his finger [me-ha-shamen] some of the oil [sheva pa'amim] seven times [lifnei YHWH] before the LORD [lifnei YHWH] [the full phrase: ve-hizzah min-ha-shamen be-etzba'o sheva pa'amim lifnei YHWH — "and he shall sprinkle from the oil with his finger seven times before the LORD"; the oil-sprinkling ritual parallels the blood-sprinkling of v.7 and of the sin-offering rite — both use the seven-times sprinkling before the LORD; this sprinkling is directed toward the presence of God (lifnei YHWH) rather than applied to the person; LXX: καὶ ῥανεῖ τῷ δακτύλῳ ἑπτάκις ἔναντι κυρίου (kai ranei tō daktylō heptakis enanti kyriou, "and shall sprinkle with the finger seven times before the Lord")]:

**17** "And from the remainder [u-mi-yeter] of the oil [ha-shamen] that is in his palm [asher be-chapo] [asher be-chapo — "which is in his palm"; chapo = "his palm" — same kap as v.15 but with possessive suffix; after sprinkling seven times before the LORD, some oil remains in the priest's palm; LXX: καὶ ἀπὸ τοῦ ἐλαίου τοῦ λοιποῦ τοῦ ἐπὶ τῆς χειρὸς αὐτοῦ (kai apo tou elaiou tou loipou tou epi tēs cheiros autou, "and from the remaining oil that is on his hand")] the priest shall put [yiten ha-kohen] on the lobe [al-tenuch] of the right ear [ozen ha-mithater ha-yemanit] of the one being cleansed and on the thumb [bohen] of his right hand [yado ha-yemanit] and on the thumb [bohen] of his right foot [raglo ha-yemanit] [the oil is applied to the same three points as the blood in v.14 — right earlobe, right thumb, right great toe — oil following blood; the interpretation: first the blood of atonement is applied, then the oil of consecration/anointing; the person is (1) atoned and (2) re-consecrated; the same pattern appeared in the priestly ordination of ch.8; LXX precisely mirrors the MT in listing the same three points: ἐπὶ τὸν λοβὸν τοῦ ὠτὸς... καὶ ἐπὶ τὸ ἄκρον τῆς χειρὸς... καὶ ἐπὶ τὸ ἄκρον τοῦ ποδός (epi ton lobon tou ōtos... kai epi to akron tēs cheiros... kai epi to akron tou podos)] on top of the blood of the guilt offering [al-dam ha-asham] [al-dam ha-asham — "on top of the blood of the guilt offering"; the oil is placed specifically on top of / over the blood spots already applied; the layering is intentional — blood first, oil second; LXX: ἐπὶ τὸν τόπον τοῦ αἵματος τῆς πλημμελείας (epi ton topon tou haimatos tēs plēmmelias, "upon the place of the blood of the trespass offering")]:

**18** "And the remainder [ve-ha-notar] [ve-ha-notar — "and the remaining / the leftover"; notar, niphal participle of yatar ("to remain, to be left over"); LXX: τὸ δὲ λοιπόν (to de loipon, "and the remaining")] of the oil [min-ha-shamen] that is in the priest's palm [asher be-chap ha-kohen] he shall put [yiten] on the head [al rosh] of the one being cleansed [ha-mithater] [al rosh ha-mithater — "upon the head of the one being cleansed"; the remaining oil from the palm (after the three-point application) is poured over the person's head; this head-anointing is the climactic act of re-consecration — the person is returned to full standing in the community; LXX: ἐπὶ τὴν κεφαλὴν τοῦ καθαριζομένου (epi tēn kephalēn tou katharizoumenou, "upon the head of the one being cleansed")] — and the priest shall make atonement [ve-khiper ha-kohen] [ve-khiper ha-kohen — "and the priest shall make atonement / shall cover / shall atone"; piel of kapar ("to cover, to atone for, to ransom"); kippur ("atonement, covering"); the verb kapar in the piel is the central verb of the Levitical atonement system — it describes what the sacrifice effects: the covering-over or removal of guilt/impurity from a person; LXX: καὶ ἐξιλάσεται ὁ ἱερεύς (kai exilasetai ho hiereus, "and the priest shall make expiation"), using ἐξιλάσκομαι (exilaskomai, "to propitiate, to expiate, to make atonement") — the standard LXX rendering of kipper] upon him [alav] before the LORD [lifnei YHWH] [lifnei YHWH — the atonement is enacted in the presence of God]:

**19** "And the priest shall make [ve-'asah ha-kohen] the sin offering [et-ha-chattat] and shall make atonement [ve-khiper] for the one being cleansed [al-ha-mithater] from his uncleanness [mi-tum'ato] [mi-tum'ato — "from his impurity / from his state of tum'ah"; tum'ah is the state of ritual uncleanness — here specifically the long-standing tum'ah of the metzora'; the sin offering effects atonement for this state; LXX: ἀπὸ τῆς ἀκαθαρσίας αὐτοῦ (apo tēs akatharsias autou, "from his impurity")] — and afterward [ve-achar] he shall slaughter [yishchat] the burnt offering [et-ha-olah] [et-ha-olah — "the burnt offering"; the olah follows the chattat; this is the standard sequence for restoration rites — sin offering first (atonement), then burnt offering (full devotion/dedication); LXX: καὶ μετὰ τοῦτο σφάξει τὸ ὁλοκαύτωμα (kai meta touto sphaxei to holokautōma, "and after this shall slaughter the burnt offering")]:

**20** "And the priest shall offer up [ve-he'elah ha-kohen] [ve-he'elah ha-kohen — "and the priest shall cause to go up / shall offer up"; hiphil of 'alah ("to go up, to ascend"): in the hiphil, "to cause to go up, to offer up" — specifically used for the burning/sending up of the burnt offering (olah), whose name derives from 'alah: it "goes up" entirely in smoke to the LORD; LXX: ἀνοίσει ὁ ἱερεύς (anoisei ho hiereus, "the priest shall bring up"), using ἀναφέρω (anapherō, "to bring up, to offer up")] the burnt offering and the grain offering [ve-et-ha-minchah] [ve-et-ha-minchah — "and the grain offering"; after the guilt offering and sin offering, the full sequence concludes with the burnt offering and grain offering; the grain offering accompanies the burnt offering; LXX: καὶ τὴν θυσίαν (kai tēn thysian, "and the offering")] upon the altar [ha-mizbechah] [ha-mizbechah — "the altar"; the definite article indicates the specific altar of the tabernacle/temple; LXX: ἐπὶ τὸ θυσιαστήριον (epi to thysiastērion, "upon the altar")] — and the priest shall make atonement [ve-khiper ha-kohen] for him [alav] and he shall be clean [ve-taher] [ve-taher — "and he shall be clean"; the climactic declaration; after the full sequence — guilt offering with blood-and-oil application rite, sin offering, burnt offering, grain offering — the person is declared clean; LXX: καὶ ἐξιλάσεται ὁ ἱερεύς καὶ καθαρὸς ἔσται (kai exilasetai ho hiereus kai katharos estai, "and the priest shall make atonement and he shall be clean")]:

**21** "And if he is poor [ve-im-dal hu'] [ve-im-dal hu' — "and if he is poor / and if he is a person of limited means"; dal from dalal ("to be low, to hang, to be weak/thin"), used in the social sense "poor, indigent, of reduced means"; this opens the third section (vv.21–32): the reduced-offering alternative for the economically disadvantaged metzora'; the halving of requirements acknowledges economic reality; LXX: ἐὰν δὲ πένης ᾖ (ean de penēs ē, "and if he is poor"), using πένης (penēs, "poor, one who works for a living") — slightly different nuance from πτωχός (ptōchos, "destitute, beggar") but similar social category] and his means [ve-yado] [ve-yado — "and his hand / his means / his capacity"; yad ("hand") is used idiomatically for "means, capacity, reach, ability": yado = "what his hand can reach = what he can afford"; LXX: καὶ ἡ χεὶρ αὐτοῦ (kai hē cheir autou, "and his hand / his means")] are insufficient [lo' tisvag] [lo' tisvag — "are not sufficient / do not reach"; tisvag from a root meaning "to suffice, to be enough, to reach"; the negative lo' tisvag = "are not enough, do not suffice"; LXX: μὴ ἱκανὴ ᾖ (mē hikanē ē, "is not sufficient"), using ἱκανός (hikanos, "sufficient, adequate, enough")] — he shall take one male lamb [ve-laqach keves echad] for a guilt offering [le-asham] as a wave offering [li-tenufah] [li-tenufah — "for a wave offering"; the single substituted lamb is still presented as a wave offering; LXX: λήμψεται ἀμνὸν ἕνα εἰς τὴν πλημμέλειαν εἰς ἐπίθεμα (lēmpsetai amnon hena eis tēn plēmeleian eis epithema)] to make atonement for him [le-khapper alav] and one-tenth [we'issaron] [we'issaron — "and one-tenth [of an ephah]"; the standard measure for a single grain offering; reduced from the three-tenths of v.10; LXX: καὶ δέκατον σεμιδάλεως (kai dekaton semidaleōs, "and a tenth of fine flour")] of fine flour [solet] mixed [balul] with oil [ba-shamen] for a grain offering [le-minchah] and a log [ve-log] of oil [shamen]:

**22** "And two turtledoves [u-shtei torim] [u-shtei torim — "and two turtledoves"; tor = turtledove (Streptopelia turtur), the smaller dove commonly used as the substitute offering for persons of limited means; throughout Leviticus, turtledoves (tor) or young pigeons (ben-yonah) are the designated substitutes for those who cannot afford sheep/goats; LXX: καὶ δύο τρυγόνας (kai dyo trygones, "and two turtledoves"), using τρυγών (trygōn, "turtledove") — the standard LXX rendering of tor] or two young pigeons [o shnei benei yonah] [o shnei benei yonah — "or two young pigeons"; benei yonah = "sons of the dove / young doves / young pigeons"; the same pair of alternatives (turtledoves or young pigeons) appears throughout Leviticus as the affordable substitute; LXX: ἢ δύο νεοσσοὺς περιστερῶν (ē dyo neossous peristerōn, "or two young doves/pigeons"), using νεοσσός (neossos, "young bird, fledgling") and περιστερά (peristera, "dove, pigeon")] — whichever [asher] his means allow [tisvag yado] [tisvag yado — "what his hand reaches / what he can afford"; positive form of the idiom used in v.21] — one shall be a sin offering [le-chattat] and one a burnt offering [ve-ha-echad le-olah] [ve-ha-echad le-olah — "and the one a burnt offering"; LXX: καὶ τὸ ἓν εἰς τὸ ὁλοκαύτωμα (kai to hen eis to holokautōma)]:

**23** "And he shall bring them [ve-hevi otam] on the eighth day [bi-yom ha-shemini] of his cleansing [le-taharat'o] to the priest [el-ha-kohen] to the entrance [el-petach] of the tent of meeting [ohel mo'ed] before the LORD [lifnei YHWH] [the poor person's offerings are brought on the same eighth day and to the same location as the full offerings of vv.10–11; the ritual timing is identical even if the offerings are reduced]:

**24** "And the priest shall take [ve-laqach ha-kohen] the guilt-offering lamb [et-keves ha-asham] and the log of oil [ve-et-log ha-shamen] and the priest shall wave them [ve-heniph otam ha-kohen] as a wave offering [tenufah] before the LORD [lifnei YHWH]:

**25** "And he shall slaughter [ve-shachat] the guilt-offering lamb [et-keves ha-asham] and the priest shall take [ve-laqach ha-kohen] from the blood [mi-dam] of the guilt offering [ha-asham] and shall put [ve-natan] it on the lobe [al-tenuch] of the right ear [ozen ha-mithater ha-yemanit] of the one being cleansed and on the thumb [bohen] of his right hand [yado ha-yemanit] and on the thumb [bohen] of his right foot [raglo ha-yemanit] [the same three-point blood-application as vv.14 and 17 — the reduced offering does not reduce the ritual precision of the application rite; every point of the full rite is replicated for the poor person]:

**26** "And the priest shall pour [ve-yitsok ha-kohen] from the oil [min-ha-shamen] onto the palm [al-kap ha-kohen] of the priest's left hand [ha-semalit] [the oil-into-left-palm procedure as in v.15]:

**27** "And the priest shall sprinkle [ve-hizzah ha-kohen] with his right forefinger [be-etzba'o ha-yemanit] from the oil [min-ha-shamen] that is on his left palm [asher al-kap ha-semalit] seven times [sheva pa'amim] before the LORD [lifnei YHWH] [identical to v.16 — the seven-times oil-sprinkling before the LORD]:

**28** "And the priest shall put [ve-natan ha-kohen] from the oil [min-ha-shamen] that is on his palm [asher al-chapo] on the lobe [al-tenuch] of the right ear [ozen ha-mithater ha-yemanit] of the one being cleansed and on the thumb [bohen] of his right hand [yado ha-yemanit] and on the thumb [bohen] of his right foot [raglo ha-yemanit] on [al] the place [meqom] of the blood of the guilt offering [dam ha-asham] [dam ha-asham — "the blood of the guilt offering"; the oil placed on top of the blood points as in v.17; the formula al meqom dam ha-asham = "upon the place of the blood of the guilt offering"; LXX: ἐπὶ τὸν τόπον τοῦ αἵματος τῆς πλημμελείας (epi ton topon tou haimatos tēs plēmmelias, "upon the place of the blood of the trespass offering") — identical to v.17]:

**29** "And the remainder [ve-ha-notar] of the oil [min-ha-shamen] that is on the priest's palm [asher al-chap ha-kohen] he shall put [yiten] on the head [al rosh] of the one being cleansed [ha-mithater] to make atonement [le-khapper] for him [alav] before the LORD [lifnei YHWH] [the head-anointing with the remaining oil, as in v.18 — the climactic re-consecration]:

**30** "And he shall make [ve-'asah] one of the turtledoves [et-achat min-ha-torim] or of the young pigeons [o min-benei ha-yonah] [o min-benei ha-yonah — "or of the young pigeons"; the alternative, as in v.22] from what his means allow [me-asher tisvag yado] [me-asher tisvag yado — "from what his hand reaches / from what he can afford"]:

**31** "[From] what his means allow [et asher-tisvag yado] — the one as a sin offering [et-ha-echad chattat] [et-ha-echad chattat — "the one as a sin offering"; the first bird is the chattat] and the one as a burnt offering [ve-et-ha-echad olah] [ve-et-ha-echad olah — "and the one as a burnt offering"; the second bird is the olah] along with the grain offering [al-ha-minchah] [al-ha-minchah — "with/upon the grain offering"; the grain offering of v.21; LXX: σὺν τῇ θυσίᾳ (syn tē thysia, "with the offering")] — and the priest shall make atonement [ve-khiper ha-kohen] for the one being cleansed [al-ha-mithater] before the LORD [lifnei YHWH]:

**32** "This [zot] is the law [torat] of the one in whom is a mark of tzara'at [asher-bo nega tzara'at] [asher-bo nega tzara'at — "in whom is a mark of tzara'at"; nega tzara'at as throughout the chapter; LXX: ἐν ᾧ οὐκ εὐπορεῖ εἰς τὴν κάθαρσιν (en hō ouk euporei eis tēn katharsin, "in whom there is not the means for his cleansing") — the LXX here inserts an explicit statement of the poverty criterion that the MT implies from v.21] whose means are not sufficient [asher lo' tisvag yado] for his cleansing [be-taharat'o] [be-taharat'o — "for his purification / in his cleansing; the formal summary of the reduced-offering legislation, vv.21–32]:

**33** And the LORD spoke [vayedabber YHWH] to Moshe and to Aharon [el-Moshe ve-el-Aharon] [el-Moshe ve-el-Aharon — "to Moshe and to Aharon"; the dual address returns here for the house-tzara'at legislation (vv.33–53); this is the same dual formula as 13:1; the priestly diagnosis (Aharon) and the legislative authority (Moshe) are both invoked; LXX: Καὶ ἐλάλησεν κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν καὶ Ααρων λέγων (Kai elalēsen kyrios pros Mōysēn kai Aarōn legōn)] saying [lemor]:

**34** "When you come [ki tavo'u] [ki tavo'u — "when you come / when you enter"; tavo'u is qal imperfect 2mp of bo' ("to come, to enter"): "when you (pl.) enter"; the plural "you" addresses the entire nation of Yisra'el prospectively; this legislation anticipates the conquest and settlement; LXX: ὅταν εἰσέλθητε (hotan eiselthēte, "whenever you enter")] into the land of Canaan [el-eretz Kena'an] [el-eretz Kena'an — "into the land of Canaan"; Kena'an — the territory the Israelites are approaching; this is an explicit prospective reference to the future settlement; house-tzara'at cannot arise in the wilderness where there are no permanent stone structures; LXX: εἰς τὴν γῆν Χανααν (eis tēn gēn Chanaan)] which I am giving [asher ani noten] to you [lakhem] [asher ani noten lakhem — "which I am giving to you"; the divine land-grant formula; ani noten = "I (the LORD) am giving"; the possession of the land is framed as a divine gift; LXX: ἣν ἐγὼ δίδωμι ὑμῖν (hēn egō didōmi hymin, "which I am giving to you")] as a possession [le-achuzzah] [le-achuzzah — "as a possession / as a holding"; achuzzah from achaz ("to grasp, to hold"), denoting a legally held property or territory; LXX: ἐν κατασχέσει (en kataschesei, "as a possession"), using κατάσχεσις (kataschesis, "possession, holding, acquisition")] — and I put [ve-natatti] [ve-natatti — "and I put / I will put"; qal perfect with consecutive-vav, 1cs: the LORD is the grammatical subject; this is the most theologically striking phrase in the entire chapter: ve-natatti nega tzara'at be-veit eretz achuzzatkhem — "and I put a mark of tzara'at in a house of your possession land"; the LORD explicitly claims to place the tzara'at-mark in the houses; this is not presented as demonic attack, natural fungal growth, or mere coincidence — the LORD himself is the agent; LXX: καὶ δώσω μώλωπα λέπρας (kai dōsō mōlōpa lepras, "and I will give a mark/wound of leprosy"), using μώλωψ (mōlōps, "stripe, welt, bruise, mark") for nega — an unusual LXX choice; μώλωψ typically means a welt or bruise from a blow, which subtly conveys the sense of divine affliction/striking] a mark [nega] of tzara'at [tzara'at] in a house [be-veit] of the land of your possession [eretz achuzzatkhem] [eretz achuzzatkhem — "the land of your possession / the land you hold"; achuzzah + possessive suffix 2mp]:

**35** "And he shall come [u-va] [u-va — "and he shall come"; qal perfect with consecutive-vav; the subject is the owner of the house; LXX: καὶ ἥξει (kai hēxei, "and shall come"), using ἥκω (hēkō, "to come, to arrive")] — the one to whom the house belongs [asher-lo ha-bayit] [asher-lo ha-bayit — "who the house belongs to / the house-owner"; the possessor, not the occupant; LXX: ᾧ ἐστὶν ἡ οἰκία (hō estin hē oikia, "whose the house is")] — and shall tell [ve-higgid] [ve-higgid — "and shall tell / report / declare"; hiphil of nagad ("to tell, to report, to declare publicly"): a formal declaration; LXX: ἀπαγγελεῖ (apangelei, "shall report, shall announce")] the priest [la-kohen] saying [lemor] [lemor — "saying"; introducing direct speech]: "Something like a mark [ke-nega] [ke-nega — "like a mark / as it were a mark"; ke- = "like, as"; the tentative formulation: the owner reports a possible nega, using the approximate ke- before making a definitive statement; this tentative language is striking — the owner does not say "there is a nega" but "there appears to be something like a nega"; perhaps to be humble before the priest's diagnosis; LXX: ὥσπερ ἁφή (hōsper haphē, "as if a mark/touch"), using ὡσπερ ("as if, as it were")] appears to me [nir'ah li] [nir'ah li — "has appeared to me / it seems to me"; niphal of ra'ah ("to be seen, to appear"), with li ("to me"): "it has appeared to me / I seem to see"; the subjective, tentative phrasing — the owner reports personal perception, not confident diagnosis; LXX: ὥσπερ ἁφὴ ἑώραταί μοι (hōsper haphē heōratai moi, "as a mark appears to me")] in the house [ba-bayit]":

**36** "And the priest shall command [ve-tzivvah ha-kohen] and they shall clear [ve-pinu] [ve-pinu — "and they shall empty / clear out"; piel of panah ("to turn, to clear, to empty"): in piel, "to clear out, to empty"; the contents of the house are cleared before the priest enters; this preserves the objects from becoming tameh by being in the house when the priest pronounces the tzara'at declaration; LXX: καὶ ἐκκενώσουσιν τὴν οἰκίαν (kai ekkenkōsousin tēn oikian, "and they shall empty the house"), using ἐκκενόω (ekkenkōō, "to empty out, to evacuate")] the house before the priest enters [bi-vo ha-kohen] [bi-vo ha-kohen — "before the coming of the priest / before the priest enters"; bi-vo = "in the entering of / at the time of the priest's entry"; infinitive construct of bo'] to look at [lir'ot et] the mark [ha-nega] — so that nothing [ve-lo'] in the house becomes unclean [yitma] [ve-lo' yitma kol-asher ba-bayit — "and everything in the house shall not become unclean"; by removing the contents before pronouncement, they avoid becoming tameh from contact with a tameh house; the priest's pronouncement is what creates the tameh status, so removing before pronouncement protects the contents; LXX: καὶ οὐ μὴ ἀκάθαρτα γένηται ὅσα ἐστὶν ἐν τῇ οἰκίᾳ (kai ou mē akatharta genētai hosa estin en tē oikia, "and all that is in the house shall not become unclean")] — and afterward [ve-achar] the priest shall enter [yavo ha-kohen] to inspect [lir'ot et] the house [et-ha-bayit]:

**37** "And he shall examine [ve-ra'ah et] the mark [ha-nega] — and behold [ve-hineh] the mark in the walls of the house [ha-nega be-qirot ha-bayit] [ha-nega be-qirot ha-bayit — "the mark on the walls of the house"; qirot = plural of qir ("wall") — the walls of the building; LXX: ἡ ἁφὴ ἐν τοῖς τοίχοις τῆς οἰκίας (hē haphē en tois toichois tēs oikias, "the mark on the walls of the house"), using τοῖχος (toichos, "wall, side-wall") for qir] — hollows [shqa'arurot] [shqa'arurot — [hollows, depressions]; shqa'arurot (plural of shqa'arura) is a hapax legomenon — appearing only here and v.39 in all of biblical Hebrew; from a root suggesting "hollowing out, sinking in, concavity"; the mark manifests as concave depressions or pitting in the wall surface; LXX: κοιλάδες (koilades, "hollows, depressions"), using κοιλάς (koilas, "hollow, valley, depression") — a precise equivalent] yellowish-green [yeraqqraqot] [yeraqqraqot — [yellowish-green]; yeraqqraqot is the intensive/reduplicative form of yaraq ("green, yellow-green"): "very-greenish / intensely green-yellow"; the color descriptor for house-tzara'at is the same palette as the garment-tzara'at of 13:49 (yeraqqraq); it is distinguished from the white (lavan) or reddish (adam) of skin-tzara'at; the greenish discoloration of stone walls likely reflects fungal growth, mold, algae, or mineral efflorescence; LXX: χλωρίζουσαι (chlorizousai, "appearing greenish"), using χλωρίζω (chlorizō, "to be green/pale") — a good equivalent, from χλωρός (chlōros, "green, pale, yellowish-green") — the same root gives us "chlorophyll" and is used in Rev 6:8 for the "pale horse" (hippos chlōros)] or reddish [adammadmot] [adammadmot — [reddish]; intensive-reduplicative form of adam ("red"): "very-reddish / intensely reddish"; parallels the admedenet of 13:49 (garment-tzara'at); LXX: ὑπέρυθροι (hyperythroi, "reddish, ruddy"), using ὑπέρυθρος (hyperythros, "rather red, reddish"), from ἐρυθρός (erythros, "red")] and their appearance [u-mar'ehen] [u-mar'ehen — "and their appearance"; feminine plural suffix referring to shqa'arurot] is lower [shafal] than the wall [min-ha-qir] [shafal min-ha-qir — "is lower/deeper than the wall"; the marks are sunken, indented into the wall surface — the same criterion of depth used for skin-tzara'at (amok in 13:3)]:

**38** "And the priest shall go out [ve-yatza' ha-kohen] [ve-yatza' ha-kohen — "and the priest shall go out"; exiting the house before pronouncing; LXX: καὶ ἐξελεύσεται ὁ ἱερεύς (kai exeleusetai ho hiereus)] from the house [min-ha-bayit] to the entrance [el-petach] of the house [ha-bayit] and shall shut up [ve-hisgir et] the house [ha-bayit] [ve-hisgir et-ha-bayit — "and shall shut/quarantine the house"; the same hisgir used for the human quarantine of ch.13: now applied to a structure; the house is "closed" as the person was "closed" — a formal quarantine; LXX: καὶ ἀποκλείσει τὴν οἰκίαν (kai apokleisei tēn oikian, "and shall shut up the house")] for seven days [shivat yamim]:

**39** "And the priest shall return [ve-shav ha-kohen] [ve-shav ha-kohen — "and the priest shall return"; qal perfect with consecutive-vav of shuv ("to return, to come back"); LXX: καὶ ἐπιστρέψει ὁ ἱερεύς (kai epistrepssei ho hiereus, "and the priest shall return")] on the seventh day [ba-yom ha-shevi'i] and shall examine [ve-ra'ah] [ve-ra'ah — "and shall examine"; same diagnostic-vision verb throughout] — and behold the mark has spread [ve-hineh pasah ha-nega] in the walls of the house [be-qirot ha-bayit] [be-qirot ha-bayit — "in the walls of the house"; same as v.37]:

**40** "And the priest shall command [ve-tzivvah ha-kohen] and they shall remove [ve-chaltshu] [ve-chaltshu — "and they shall remove / pull out / extract"; piel of chalats ("to pull out, to extract, to remove"), in the piel intensive: to tear out, to remove by force; LXX: ἐξελοῦσιν (exelousin, "they shall remove"), using ἐξαιρέω (exaireō, "to take out, to remove, to extract")] the stones [et-ha-avanim] [et-ha-avanim — "the stones"; the structure is of stone construction; in Canaan, houses were typically built of limestone; LXX: τοὺς λίθους (tous lithous, "the stones")] in which is the mark [asher-bam ha-nega] [asher-bam ha-nega — "in which the mark is / in which the tzara'at is"; the infected stones specifically, not all stones] and shall cast them [ve-hishlichu otam] [ve-hishlichu otam — "and they shall throw/cast them"; hiphil of shalach ("to throw, to cast") in the intensive sense; LXX: ἐκβαλοῦσιν αὐτοὺς (ekbalousin autous, "they shall cast them out"), using ἐκβάλλω (ekballō, "to throw out, to cast out")] to outside the city [el-michutz la-'ir] [el-michutz la-'ir — "to outside the city"; michutz + la-'ir ("outside the city"); the infected stones go to an impure place outside the city — parallel to the disposal of sin-offering remains and tzara'at-tameh persons outside the camp; the contaminated material must exit the community; LXX: ἔξω τῆς πόλεως (exō tēs poleōs, "outside the city"), using πόλις (polis, "city") for 'ir] to an unclean place [el-maqom tameh] [el-maqom tameh — "to an unclean place"; a designated area for ritually defiling refuse; LXX: εἰς τόπον ἀκάθαρτον (eis topon akatharon, "to an unclean place")]:

**41** "And the house [ve-et-ha-bayit] shall be scraped [yiqtzoa'] [yiqtzoa' — "shall be scraped"; qal imperfect passive of qatsa' ("to scrape, to shave, to pare"); the walls of the house are scraped — the surface material, plaster, mortar, and any fungal infiltration is stripped from the walls; LXX: καὶ τὴν οἰκίαν ξύσουσιν (kai tēn oikian xysousin, "and they shall scrape the house"), using ξύω (xyō, "to scrape, to shave")] on the inside [mi-bayit] [mi-bayit — "from within / on the inside"; the interior walls are scraped; LXX: ἔσωθεν (esōthen, "from within")] all around [saviv] [saviv — "all around / on every side"; LXX: κύκλῳ (kyklō, "in a circle, all around")] and they shall pour out [ve-shafkhu] [ve-shafkhu — "and they shall pour out / shall empty out"; piel of shafak ("to pour out, to shed"), intensive: "shall pour off"; the scraped material — plaster dust, fungal matter, wall scrapings — is poured out or dumped; LXX: ἐκχεοῦσιν τὸν χοῦν (ekcheousin ton choun, "they shall pour out the dust"), using χοῦς (chous, "dust, earth, dirt")] the dust they scraped off [et-he'afar asher hiqtsu'u] [et-he'afar asher hiqtsu'u — "the dust/dirt which they scraped"; he'afar = "the dust, the scraped-off material"; hiqtsu'u = piel 3mp of qatsa', "they scraped"; LXX: ὃν ἐξέξεσαν (hon exexesan, "which they scraped off")] to outside the city [el-michutz la-'ir] to an unclean place [el-maqom tameh] [the same disposal location as the removed stones in v.40]:

**42** "And they shall take [ve-laqchu] other [acheret] stones [avanim] [avanim acheret — "other stones"; new, uninfected stones to replace the removed infected ones; LXX: λίθους ἑτέρους (lithous heterous, "other stones")] and shall bring them [ve-hevi'u] to in place of [el-tachat] [el-tachat — "to in the place of / as a replacement for"; tachat ("under, beneath, in the place of") — the substitution preposition; LXX: ἀντὶ τῶν λίθων (anti tōn lithōn, "instead of the stones"), using ἀντί (anti, "instead of, in place of")] the stones [ha-avanim] — and other dust [ve-afar acher] [ve-afar acher — "and other plaster/dust"; fresh plastering material; LXX: καὶ χοῦν ἕτερον (kai choun heteron, "and other dust/earth")] shall he take [yiqqach] and shall plaster [ve-tipach] [ve-tipach — "and shall plaster / shall re-plaster"; qal imperfect of tapach ("to plaster, to coat with plaster, to daub"); LXX: λεπίσει (lepisei, "shall plaster/coat"), using λεπίζω (lepizō, "to peel, to coat, to plaster")] the house [et-ha-bayit]:

**43** "And if the mark [ve-im yashuv ha-nega] returns [yashuv] [yashuv — "returns / comes back"; qal imperfect of shuv ("to return"); the tzara'at re-emerging after the treatment; LXX: ἐὰν δὲ ἐπιστρέψῃ ἡ ἁφή (ean de epistrepsē hē haphē, "if the mark returns")] and breaks out [u-parach] [u-parach — "and breaks out"; parach qal: "to break out, to blossom, to spread"; LXX: καὶ ἀνθήσῃ (kai anthēsē, "and blossoms/breaks out")] in the house [ba-bayit] after [achar] the stones have been removed [chalots et-ha-avanim] [chalots et-ha-avanim — "the removing of the stones"; infinitive absolute + object, serving as a temporal marker: "after the removing of the stones"; LXX: μετὰ τὸ ἐξαιρεθῆναι τοὺς λίθους (meta to exairethēnai tous lithous, "after the stones being removed")] and after [ve-achar] the house has been scraped [hiqtzot et-ha-bayit] [hiqtzot et-ha-bayit — "the scraping of the house"; infinitive construct of qatsa' + definite object] and after [ve-achar] it has been plastered [hippoch] [hippoch — "the plastering"; qal infinitive construct of tipach (plaster)] [the sequence of remediation in vv.40–42]:

**44** "And the priest shall come [u-va ha-kohen] and shall examine [ve-ra'ah] and behold [ve-hineh] the mark has spread [pasah ha-nega] in the house [ba-bayit] [pasah ha-nega ba-bayit — "the mark has spread in the house"; the return and spreading of the tzara'at after full treatment is conclusive: the house is condemned] — it [is] a persistent tzara'at [tzara'at mameret] [tzara'at mameret — "malignant tzara'at / aggressive tzara'at / persistent tzara'at"; mameret is a hapax legomenon in the adjective form — from marar ("to be bitter, to be strong/aggressive") or from the root marad ("to rebel")? Most interpreters derive it from marar, translating "stubborn, persistent, malignant, resistant"; the house-tzara'at that returns after full treatment is deemed mameret — unresponsive, persistent; LXX: λέπρα ἔμμονος (lepra emmonos, "persistent/ingrained leprosy"), using ἔμμονος (emmonos, "lasting, persistent, deep-seated") — a good equivalent for mameret in the sense of "stubbornly persistent"] — it [is] unclean [tameh hu'] [tameh hu' — the verdict of tum'ah]:

**45** "And he shall tear down [ve-natatz] [ve-natatz — "and shall tear down / shall demolish"; qal imperfect of natats ("to tear down, to demolish, to pull apart"): the irreversible act of demolition; LXX: καὶ καθελοῦσιν τὴν οἰκίαν (kai kathelousin tēn oikian, "and they shall demolish the house"), using καθαιρέω (kathaireō, "to pull down, to demolish, to destroy")] the house [et-ha-bayit] — its stones [et-avaneha] [et-avaneha — "its stones"; the stones of the demolished house; LXX: τοὺς λίθους αὐτῆς (tous lithous autēs, "its stones")] and its timbers [ve-et-etseha] [ve-et-etseha — "and its timbers / its wooden beams"; etsim = "wood, timber, beams" — the wooden structural elements of the house; LXX: καὶ τὴν ξυλείαν αὐτῆς (kai tēn xyleian autēs, "and its timber"), using ξυλεία (xyleia, "woodwork, timber, lumber")] and all the plaster [ve-et kol-afar ha-bayit] [ve-et kol-afar ha-bayit — "and all the plaster/dust of the house"; afar = "dust, plaster, earth"; LXX: καὶ πάντα τὸν χοῦν τῆς οἰκίας (kai panta ton choun tēs oikias, "and all the dust/earth of the house")] — and he shall carry them out [ve-hotzi el] to outside [michutz] the city [la-'ir] to an unclean place [el-maqom tameh] [el-maqom tameh — the designated unclean disposal area, as in vv.40–41; the entirety of the demolished house — stones, timber, plaster — is removed from the city]:

**46** "And anyone who enters [ve-ha-ba el] [ve-ha-ba el — "and the one who enters into"; qal participle of bo', "the one who comes/enters"] the house [ha-bayit] during all the days [kol-yemei] [kol-yemei — "all the days"; throughout the quarantine period] that it has been shut up [hisgir oto] [hisgir oto — "it was shut/quarantined"; referring to the quarantine period; LXX: ἐν ᾗ ἀπεκλείσθη (en hē apekleisthē, "in which it was shut up")] shall be unclean [ve-tame] until the evening [ad ha-erev] [ad ha-erev — "until the evening"; tum'ah contracted by entering a tameh house lasts only until evening — a lighter form of impurity than skin-tzara'at; LXX: ἀκάθαρτος ἔσται ἕως ἑσπέρας (akathartos estai heōs hesperas, "shall be unclean until evening")]:

**47** "And anyone who lies down [ve-ha-shokhev] [ve-ha-shokhev — "and the one who lies down"; qal participle of shakav ("to lie down, to recline")] in the house [ba-bayit] shall wash his clothes [yekabbes et-begadav] [yekabbes et-begadav — "shall wash his garments"; laundering required for anyone who sleeps in the quarantined house; more intensive contact = more intensive decontamination requirement; LXX: καὶ ὁ κοιμώμενος ἐν αὐτῇ πλυνεῖ τὰ ἱμάτια αὐτοῦ (kai ho koimōmenos en autē plynei ta himatia autou, "and the one sleeping in it shall wash his garments")] — and anyone who eats [ve-ha-okhel] [ve-ha-okhel — "and the one who eats"; qal participle of akhal ("to eat")] in the house [ba-bayit] shall wash his clothes [yekabbes et-begadav] [the same laundering requirement for anyone eating in the quarantined house — the principle of contact-contamination applies to activities involving sustained presence; LXX: καὶ ὁ ἐσθίων ἐν αὐτῇ πλυνεῖ τὰ ἱμάτια αὐτοῦ (kai ho esthiōn en autē plynei ta himatia autou, "and the one eating in it shall wash his garments")]:

**48** "And if the priest comes in [ve-im bo' yavo ha-kohen] [ve-im bo' yavo ha-kohen — "and if entering the priest enters"; bo' yavo = infinitive absolute + finite verb, emphatic: "if truly entering he enters / if he does indeed enter"; the emphasis stresses the priest's actual return visit; LXX: ἐὰν δὲ εἰσελθὼν εἰσέλθῃ ὁ ἱερεύς (ean de eiselthōn eiselthē ho hiereus, "if entering the priest enters")] and examines [ve-ra'ah] and behold the mark has not spread [ve-hineh lo' pasah ha-nega] in the house [ba-bayit] after [achar] the house was plastered [hippach et-ha-bayit] [hippach et-ha-bayit — "the plastering of the house; the completion of the remediation] — the priest shall declare the house clean [ve-tihar ha-kohen et-ha-bayit] [ve-tihar ha-kohen et-ha-bayit — "and the priest shall declare the house clean"; the priest's declaration of tahor applies to the house, not merely to a person — the structure itself receives a ritual verdict; LXX: καθαριεῖ ὁ ἱερεὺς τὴν οἰκίαν (kathariei ho hiereus tēn oikian, "the priest shall declare the house clean")] for the mark has been healed [nirpa ha-nega] [nirpa ha-nega — "the mark has been healed"; niphal of rapa', passive: "it was healed"; the same verb used for the healed human tzara'at at v.3]:

**49** "And he shall take [ve-laqach] to cleanse [le-chatte'] [le-chatte' — "to un-sin / to purify / to de-sin"; piel infinitive of chata', the denominative verb from chattat; in the piel, chata' means "to de-sin, to purify from sin-impurity, to perform the sin-purification rite" — not "to sin" (which is the qal) but "to cleanse from sin / to perform the chattat rite"; LXX: ἁγνίσαι τὴν οἰκίαν (hagnisai tēn oikian, "to purify/consecrate the house"), using ἁγνίζω (hagnizō, "to purify, to consecrate, to make holy")] the house [et-ha-bayit] two birds [shtei tzipporim] [shtei tzipporim — "two birds"; the same two-bird rite as vv.4–7 for the human metzora', now applied to the house; the structural parallel is deliberate and instructive: person and house undergo the same restoration rite] and cedar wood [ve-etz erez] and scarlet [yarn] [usheni tola'at] and hyssop [ve-ezov] [the same four materials as v.4]:

**50** "And he shall slaughter [ve-shachat] one bird [et-ha-tzippor ha-achat] over an earthen vessel [al-keli cheres] over living water [al-mayim chayyim] [exact repetition of v.5; the ritual procedure for the house is a precise mirror of the human rite]:

**51** "And he shall take [ve-laqach] the cedar wood [et-etz ha-erez] and the hyssop [ve-et-ha-ezov] and the scarlet yarn [ve-et sheni ha-tola'at] [note the order is slightly rearranged: v.6 had cedar, scarlet, hyssop; v.51 has cedar, hyssop, scarlet — a minor variation within the same triad] and the living bird [ve-et ha-tzippor ha-chayyah] and shall dip [ve-taval] them [otam] in the blood of the slaughtered bird [be-dam ha-tzippor ha-shechutah] and in the living water [u-ve-mayim ha-chayyim] [u-ve-mayim ha-chayyim — "and in the living water"; the blood-water mixture; the formula here adds ve-u-ve-mayim ha-chayyim explicitly; LXX: καὶ ἐν τῷ ὕδατι τῷ ζῶντι (kai en tō hydati tō zōnti, "and in the living water")] and shall sprinkle [ve-hizzah] the house [et-ha-bayit] seven times [sheva pa'amim] [the seven-times sprinkling applied to the house structure, paralleling the seven-times sprinkling on the person in v.7]:

**52** "And he shall purify [ve-chitta'] [ve-chitta' — "and he shall purify / shall perform the de-sin rite"; piel of chata', as in v.49; LXX: καὶ ἁγνίσει (kai hagnissei, "and shall purify")] the house [et-ha-bayit] with the blood of the bird [be-dam ha-tzippor] [be-dam ha-tzippor — "with the blood of the bird"; the slaughtered bird's blood used as the purificatory agent; LXX: τῷ αἵματι τοῦ ὀρνιθίου (tō haimati tou ornithiou, "with the blood of the small bird")] and with the living water [u-ve-mayim ha-chayyim] and with the living bird [ve-et-ha-tzippor ha-chayyah] and with the cedar wood [u-ve-etz ha-erez] and with the hyssop [u-ve-ha-ezov] and with the scarlet [u-ve-sheni] [the instruments of purification enumerated: blood + water + living bird + cedar + hyssop + scarlet; the living bird participates as instrument, not just as that-which-is-released]:

**53** "And he shall release [ve-shillach] [ve-shillach — "and shall release / send away"; same verb as v.7 for the person's rite; LXX: ἐξαποστελεῖ (exapostelei, "shall release")] the living bird [et-ha-tzippor ha-chayyah] to outside the city [el-michutz la-'ir] [el-michutz la-'ir — "to outside the city"; note the city replaces "the camp" of v.7 — the house-tzara'at legislation uses urban terminology (city, 'ir) rather than wilderness terminology (camp, machaneh), because it applies to the future settlement in Canaan; LXX: ἔξω τῆς πόλεως (exō tēs poleōs, "outside the city")] into the open field [el-penei ha-sadeh] [el-penei ha-sadeh — "over the face of the field / into the open field"; same as v.7; LXX: εἰς τὸ πεδίον (eis to pedion, "into the plain")] — and he shall make atonement [ve-khiper] for the house [al-ha-bayit] [al-ha-bayit — "for the house"; the house, like the person, requires atonement; kipper is applied to a structure; LXX: καὶ ἐξιλάσεται περὶ τῆς οἰκίας (kai exilasetai peri tēs oikias, "and shall make expiation for the house")] and it shall be clean [ve-taher] [ve-taher — "and it shall be clean"; the house receives the tahor verdict; LXX: καὶ καθαρὸν ἔσται (kai katharon estai, "and it shall be clean")]:

---

**54** "This [zot] is the law [ha-torah] for every mark of tzara'at [le-khol-nega ha-tzara'at] [le-khol-nega ha-tzara'at — "for every mark/affliction of tzara'at"; the colophon begins: this summary encompasses the entire tzara'at legislation of chs.13–14; LXX: οὗτος ὁ νόμος πάσης ἁφῆς λέπρας (houtos ho nomos pasēs haphēs lepras, "this is the law of every mark of leprosy")] and for the neteq [ve-la-neteq] [ve-la-neteq — "and for the neteq"; neteq = the scall/scalp-tzara'at of the head (13:29–37): the word preserved untranslated in this summary refers back to the specific category of ch.13; LXX: καὶ σημασίας (kai sēmasias, "and of markings")]:

**55** "And for the tzara'at of the garment [ve-le-tzara'at ha-beged] [ve-le-tzara'at ha-beged — "and for the tzara'at of the garment"; the garment-tzara'at of 13:47–59; LXX: καὶ λέπρας ἱματίου (kai lepras himatiou, "and leprosy of a garment")] and for the house [ve-la-bayit] [ve-la-bayit — "and for the house"; the house-tzara'at of 14:33–53]:

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations

**Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 66r–66v:** Both folio pages are clearly legible in the source images. The text is written in a clean Ashkenazic square script with Tiberian vocalization (niqqud) and cantillation marks (te'amim) throughout. The four-column layout of folio 66r shows the chapter running from approximately the middle of the first column through the end of the fourth. Folio 66v continues through to the colophon at v.57. Paragraph markers (parashot) are visible at the major section breaks: before v.1 (chapter opening), before v.33 (house-tzara'at), and before v.54 (colophon/summary). The section divisions align with the four natural literary units of the chapter. The Masoretic marginal notes (masorah parva) are visible in the outer margins; no significant text variants are apparent from the images. The word ha-metzora' (v.2) appears as a single construct-form technical term consistently throughout. The hapax legomenon shqa'arurot (v.37) is clearly vocalized in the codex as ש ַׁ ק ַּ עְ רֻ רוֹת, confirming the traditional pointing.

**Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 99–100:** The Vaticanus text is clearly legible in uncial script (large, separate capital letters), without word spacing or punctuation except for minor scribal marks. Page 99 opens with a decorated initial kappa (K) for the beginning of a major section (Καὶ ἐλάλησεν, "And the LORD spoke"), with an ornamental headpiece. This decorated opening is visible in the source image — the large blue-and-gold illuminated K confirms this is a major section opening in the LXX manuscript tradition. The chapter proceeds in three columns. Page 100 continues the house-tzara'at legislation and contains the colophon text at the end. The chapter headings/markers in Vaticanus use the standard Greek κεφάλαιον (chapter) notation in the margins. There are no visible lacunae in either page.

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **tzara'at** (vv.2, 3, 7, 34 et passim): Preserved untranslated throughout, as established for ch.13. The LXX's λέπρα/λεπρός (lepra/lepros) gave the traditional "leprosy" rendering, but this does not correspond to modern Hansen's disease. The Hebrew term covers a spectrum of skin conditions recognized by appearance alone.

2. **metzora'** (vv.2, 3, 32): "One afflicted with tzara'at" — the qal passive participle retained as a technical term at first occurrence; abbreviated as "the one being cleansed" (ha-mithater) in later parallel constructions. Not "the leper."

3. **ve-yatza' ha-kohen** (v.3): "And the priest shall go out" — emphasized in translation because the priest's movement outward to the excluded person is theologically significant. The priest comes to the person; the person does not approach the sanctuary. This reverses the usual direction of approach in Levitical legislation.

4. **shtei-tzipporim chayyot tehorot** (v.4): "Two live clean birds" — all three attributes (two, live, clean) are essential: two birds (for the split-bird rite), alive (one will be released alive), clean (ritually permitted species). The specific species is not named; the text says only tzipporim ("birds") and the requirement is only that they be tahor (from the permitted list of ch.11).

5. **etz erez / sheni tola'at / ezov** (v.4): The triad "cedar wood, scarlet yarn, hyssop" appears identically in Num 19:6 (red heifer ceremony). Its significance in both contexts remains debated. The cedar/hyssop contrast (great tree / humble herb) may be intentional — the combination of high-status and low-status plants. The tola'at (worm/insect from which scarlet dye is extracted) makes the "scarlet yarn" a product of a living organism, fitting the life/death binary of the rite.

6. **mayim chayyim** (vv.5, 6, 50, 51, 52): "Living water" — consistently rendered literally. This is spring water or running water, not cistern/standing water. The "living" (chayyim) quality of the water parallels the "living" (chayyah) bird — both the water and the bird are vital, animate, and free-flowing.

7. **ve-taval** (v.6): "And he shall dip them" — taval is the verb of immersion/dipping, the root that gives the later rabbinic term tevilah (immersion/bathing for purification). The LXX renders it with βάπτω (baptō), the root of "baptize." The dipping of the living bird into the blood-and-water mixture is a transferal rite: the living bird absorbs the blood (atonement) and the water (purity), then carries both into the open field when released.

8. **ve-shillach... al-penei ha-sadeh** (v.7): "And shall release... over the open field" — shillach in the piel is the deliberate act of sending/releasing; al-penei ha-sadeh ("over the face of the field") echoes the phrase in Gen 1 for the "face of the deep" and "face of the waters." The bird is released into the created order — the open natural world — carrying the impurity away.

9. **tenuch ozen** (vv.14, 17, 25, 28): "Lobe of the ear" — tenuch is an anatomical hapax used only in this ritual context and in the ordination of ch.8. The specific term (as opposed to the generic ozen/"ear") suggests precise anatomical awareness. The same three points — right earlobe, right thumb, right great toe — appear in 8:23–24 for the priestly ordination blood-rite, making the restoration of the metzora' a recapitulation of priestly consecration.

10. **asham** (vv.12, 13, 14, 17, 21, 24, 25, 28): "Guilt offering" — the asham is the first and central offering of the restoration sequence, preceding even the chattat. This is unusual: the standard order in Leviticus is chattat then olah, with asham for specific categories of guilt. The metzora's restoration begins with the asham, suggesting the condition of tzara'at-impurity is understood as a form of trespass against the sacred realm requiring the specific asham-atonement before the general atonement of the chattat.

11. **ve-natatti nega tzara'at** (v.34): "And I put a mark of tzara'at" — the first-person divine causation is stark and unambiguous. The LORD is not merely permitting or predicting house-tzara'at; the grammar (ve-natatti, waw-consecutive perfect 1cs of natan: "and I will put / I put") assigns active causation to YHWH. This is theologically remarkable: the same God who commands the purification rites is the one who places the tzara'at requiring them. The LXX softens this slightly with δώσω ("I will give") rather than a more volitional word, but both grammars convey divine agency.

12. **shqa'arurot** (v.37): "Hollows" — a hapax legomenon appearing only in Lev 14:37 and 39. The context makes "hollow depressions, concavities" the clear meaning. The marks in the wall are not raised but sunken — indented depressions of yellowish-green or reddish coloring. This description fits fungal/mold infestation in stone walls quite precisely: the mycelium colonizes and degrades stone, causing discoloration and surface crumbling.

13. **tzara'at mameret** (v.44): "Persistent tzara'at" — mameret is a hapax (appears once in the Bible). From marar ("to be bitter/strong") or a related root. Rendered "persistent" to convey the sense of a condition that resists treatment and reasserts itself — a malignant, stubborn, returning tzara'at. The condemned house must be entirely demolished.

14. **le-horot** (v.57): "To teach" — the final word (before the formal colophon formula) defines the purpose of the entire tzara'at legislation: le-horot, "to teach," from the root of torah. The legislation is instruction in when things are tameh and when tahor — a structure for navigating the boundary between the clean and unclean, the holy and the common. This purpose-statement is the torah of tzara'at's self-description.

15. **The poor person's alternative (vv.21–32)**: One of the most explicit acknowledgments of economic inequality in Levitical legislation. The full rite (vv.10–20) requires three lambs (two males, one female) and three-tenths of fine flour. The reduced rite (vv.21–32) substitutes one lamb and two birds for the animal offerings, and one-tenth of fine flour for the grain offering. Critically, the blood-and-oil application rite (earlobe, thumb, great toe) remains fully intact — the precision of the restoration ritual is maintained even at reduced cost. The reduction applies only to the scale of the sacrifice, not to any ritual step.
